---
title: "2.3 if Statements"
subtitle: "Part 1: One-Way Selection and Relational Ifs"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Selection changes sequential execution

<span class="term">If statements</span> (条件语句) are found in all programming languages as a way to choose between different paths in an algorithm.

An if statement is a type of <span class="term">selection</span> statement that changes the sequential execution. It affects the <span class="term">flow of control</span> by executing different segments of code based on the value of a <span class="term">Boolean expression</span>.

## If Blocks and Java Ifs

### App Inventor, AP CSP, and Java

If you took an AP CSP course or used a block programming language like Scratch, you've probably seen if blocks or statements before.

![Comparison of App Inventor if block, AP CSP ifs, and Java if statements](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-3-if-statements/BlocksIfComparison.png){width="100%"}

## One-Way Selection

### Body runs only when the Boolean expression is true

A <span class="term">one-way selection</span> (if statement) is used when there is a segment of code, called the <span class="term">body</span> of the if statement, to execute under a certain condition.

If the Boolean expression is <span class="mark">true</span>, the body is executed. If the Boolean expression is <span class="mark">false</span>, the body is skipped and the program continues with the next statement after the if statement.

![The order that statements execute in a conditional](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-3-if-statements/Condition.png){fig-align="center" width="28%"}

## One-Way Syntax

### Braces group the body

The open curly brace `{` and close curly brace `}` are used to group a block of statements together as the body of the if statement.

It is recommended to always put in the curly braces even if you have just one statement under the if statement. The AP exam uses curly braces.

## One-Way Syntax Patterns

### Single statement and block forms

```java
// A single if statement
if (boolean expression)
    Do statement;

// Or a single if with {}
if (boolean expression)
{
   Do statement;
}

// A block if statement: { } required
if (boolean expression)
{
   Do Statement1;
   Do Statement2;
   Do StatementN;
}
```

## If Statement Note

### No semicolon after the Boolean expression

There is no semicolon `;` at the end of the Boolean expression in an if statement, even if it is the end of that line.

The semicolon goes at the end of the whole if statement, often on the next line. Or `{ }` are used to mark the beginning and end of the block of code under the if condition.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: if-raining`

Textbook prompt: The variable `isRaining` is a Boolean variable that is either true or false. If it is true then the message `Take an umbrella!` will be printed and then execution will continue with the next statement which will print `Drive carefully`.

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        boolean isRaining = true;
        if (isRaining)
        {
            System.out.println("Take an umbrella!");
        }
        System.out.println("Drive carefully");
    }
}
```
:::

## Expected Output

### `if-raining`

The Runestone test expects this `main` output:

```text
Take an umbrella!
Drive carefully
```

Execution continues after the if statement whether the body ran or was skipped.

## Trace Checkpoint

### `fillintheblank:: iffalseOutput`

Textbook prompt: Try changing the code above to `boolean isRaining = false;`. What will it print?

Student response:

```text
____________________________
```

## Trace Answer

### `fillintheblank:: iffalseOutput`

Correct response:

```text
Drive carefully
```

Reasoning: If the Boolean is false, Java skips the print statement inside the if body and continues with the next statement.

## Relational Operators in If Statements

### Conditions often compare values

Most if statements have a Boolean condition that uses relational operators like `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, as in the last lesson.

The condition may compare a variable to a constant value, another variable, or an arithmetic expression.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: if-relational`

Textbook prompt: Run the following active code a couple times until you see all the possible outputs. It prints out whether a random number is positive or equal to `0`. Add another if statement that tests if it is a negative number.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: if-relational`

::: {.code-scroll .compact}
```java
public class TestNumbers
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // Get a random number from -10 up to 10.
        int number = (int) (Math.random() * 21) - 10;
        System.out.println("The number is " + number);

        // is it positive?
        if (number > 0)
        {
            System.out.println(number + " is positive!");
        }
        // is it 0?
        if (number == 0)
        {
            System.out.println(number + " is zero!");
        }
        // is it negative?
        // Add another if statement

    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: if-relational`

Runestone repeatedly runs `main` until it sees these cases:

::: {.tight-list}
- a positive number output that contains `positive`
- zero output that contains `zero`
- a negative number output that contains `negative`
:::

Expected output pattern for the negative case after the missing if is added:

```text
The number is -3
-3 is negative!
```

## Common Equality Mistake

### Use `==`, not `=`, in a condition

A common mistake in if statements is using `=` instead of `==` in the condition by mistake.

You should always <span class="mark">use `==`, not `=`</span>, in the condition of an if statement to test a variable.

One equal sign `=` assigns a value to a variable, and two equal signs `==` test if a variable has a certain value.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: mcq-if-trace`

Consider the following code segment. What is printed as a result of executing the code segment?

```java
int x = 3;
if (x > 2)
{
   x = x * 2;
}
if (x > 4)
{
   x = 0;
}
System.out.print(x);
```

Options: `3`, `6`, `0`, `4`, `The code will not compile`.

## Answer Reasoning

### `mchoice:: mcq-if-trace`

Correct answer: `0`.

Reasoning: If `x` is greater than `2`, it is doubled. Then that result is greater than `4`, so `x` is set to `0` in the second if statement.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define an `if statement` as selection that changes sequential execution
- explain when a one-way selection body runs and when it is skipped
- use braces to group the body of an if statement
- avoid a semicolon immediately after the Boolean expression
- use `==`, not `=`, when testing equality in a condition
- trace consecutive if statements in source order
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 2

[Next: if-else and common errors →](2-3-part-2-if-else-and-common-errors.html)
